question: How would I build a simple subtractive synth: white noise through a resonant filter, with an envelope controlling the filter cutoff?
reference_answer: Use [noise~] as the source, feed it into [vcf~] (a resonant bandpass filter whose center frequency is set by a signal into its right inlet), and drive that frequency signal with [line~] to sweep the cutoff over time as an envelope.
notes: Multi-object synthesis: requires correctly combining 3 objects' documented inlet behavior into one coherent signal chain, not restating one doc entry.

question: How do I build a monophonic synth voice that responds to MIDI note-in, including handling note-off?
reference_answer: Use [notein] to receive MIDI note/velocity/channel, [stripnote] to filter to note-on-only messages (or check velocity for note-off), and convert to frequency with [mtof] before driving an oscillator; [makenote] can pair note-on/note-off with a duration for simple sequencing.
notes: Multi-object synthesis across MIDI-handling objects; note-off handling is a common source of invented behavior.

question: How can I record a short sample into a table and play it back at a different pitch?
reference_answer: Record with [tabwrite~] into a named array, then play back with [tabread4~] (which supports fractional/interpolated read indices) driven by a phasor or line ramping at a different rate than the original sample rate to change pitch.
notes: Combines record and playback objects; tests whether the model invents specific playback-rate math not in the docs vs. correctly flagging it as general technique.

question: How would I build a simple 8-step sequencer that reads frequencies from a table and triggers a tone on each step?
reference_answer: Use [metro] to generate a steady pulse, a counter (e.g. [+ 1] with a [mod 8] wrapped through a float storage, or [select]/[route] to branch by step) to track the current step index, [tabread] to look up the frequency for that step, and send the result to an oscillator.
notes: Requires synthesizing a counter mechanism the docs don't spell out as one recipe; good test of whether the model over-fabricates specific object names for the counter.

question: When would I use [vline~] instead of [line~], and what's the practical difference?
reference_answer: [line~] generates a linear ramp from one message but restarts if a new ramp is sent before the current one finishes, and its timing can be disrupted by DSP block boundaries. [vline~] supports scheduling multiple queued ramp segments with delays in a single message and is more precise about timing since it isn't tied to block boundaries the same way, making it better for tightly-timed envelope segments.
notes: Comparison question forcing the model to hold two chunks in tension rather than reciting one; a common fabrication risk is inventing a precise mechanism for the timing difference.

question: How would I build a bucket brigade delay-style effect? I'd like a variable delay line, a lowpass filter in the feedback loop, a feedback amount setting, and for extra points, a limiter on the output and an interpolating pitch-shift effect when the delay time changes.
reference_answer: Write into a delay line with [delwrite~], read back with [delread~] (or [delread4~]/[vd~] specifically for smooth interpolated pitch-shift effects when the delay time is changed continuously, since [delread~] alone is non-interpolating). Route the output through [lop~] to darken repeats, scale it by a feedback gain, and sum it back into [delwrite~]'s input to close the loop. [clip~] can serve as a simple hard limiter on the final output, since vanilla Pd has no dedicated limiter object. Non-recirculating vs. recirculating delay networks have a documented one-block minimum delay / order-of-execution subtlety that matters once feedback is involved.
notes: Simple effect with many opportunities to improve on a bare delay line (limiter, feedback filtering, interpolated pitch-shift). Tests whether the model reaches for delread4~/vd~ specifically (correct) vs. hand-waving pitch-shift on top of plain delread~, and whether it invents a nonexistent limiter~ object instead of reasoning to clip~.
